question: Configure etcd to use encryption at rest with the AES-CBC provider. Ensure existing secrets are re-encrypted after the change.
Restrict access to the Kubernetes API server using a firewall to allow only a specific management CIDR. Also, ensure the API server uses a non-default port.
Implement a network plugin that supports NetworkPolicies (e.g., Calico or Cilium) and verify that a default-deny ingress policy is effective.
Configure the Kubernetes API server to log all request bodies for 'Secrets' in the 'prod' namespace to a local file. Set maximum retention to 10 days.
Harden the Kubernetes dashboard deployment by disabling the 'Skip' login option and ensuring it uses a dedicated ServiceAccount with minimal permissions.
Use 'kube-bench' to identify misconfigurations in the API server and Kubelet. Remedial actions should include disabling anonymous authentication on the Kubelet.
A ServiceAccount 'data-reader' has been granted 'cluster-admin'. Revoke this and grant it only 'get' and 'list' permissions on 'Pods' in the 'finance' namespace.
The API server currently allows anonymous access. Disable '--anonymous-auth' and ensure that the Kubelet is also configured to reject anonymous requests.
Implement NodeRestriction admission controller to ensure Kubelets can only modify their own Node and Pod objects.
The 'default' ServiceAccount in all namespaces has too much power. Configure all 'default' ServiceAccounts to not automount tokens into pods.
Upgrade a Kubernetes cluster from v1.28 to v1.29 to mitigate a critical security vulnerability in the API server.
Restrict traffic to the etcd cluster so that only the API server can communicate with it. Verify with client certificates.

Rotate all service account signing keys to invalidate potentially compromised tokens.
Apply an AppArmor profile to a container 'web-app' to restrict write access to the entire filesystem except for /var/www/html/uploads.
Configure a Pod to use a custom Seccomp profile located at /var/lib/kubelet/seccomp/profiles/audit.json to audit all syscalls.
A pod needs to run with specific capabilities. Drop ALL capabilities and add only 'NET_BIND_SERVICE'.
Identify and remove unnecessary OS-level packages and services on a Kubernetes worker node to reduce the attack surface.
Harden the SSH daemon on all nodes by disabling password authentication and root login.
Use 'strace' to identify which syscalls are being used by a running process in a pod and then create a Seccomp profile to block everything else.
Configure a Pod to use a read-only root filesystem and mount a writable emptyDir volume for logs at /var/log/app.
Verify that all binaries in /usr/bin on a worker node are owned by root and have appropriate permissions (755).
Implement kernel-level hardening by setting 'net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter' to 1 via sysctl to prevent IP spoofing.
Create a NetworkPolicy in the 'app' namespace that allows ingress to pods labeled 'role: db' only from pods labeled 'role: api' on port 5432.
Configure a Deployment so that its pods do not have access to the Kubernetes API server by setting 'automountServiceAccountToken: false'.
Label the 'production' namespace to enforce the 'restricted' Pod Security Standard (PSS).
Use an admission controller (e.g., Gatekeeper) to block any Pod that attempts to use a hostPath volume.

Configure a Pod to use the 'RuntimeDefault' seccomp profile to provide a baseline set of allowed syscalls.
